1: Psalm 131, attributed to David, is one of the shortest psalms in the Psalter and perhaps the most concentrated in its spiritual achievement. It models the inner posture that the entire Songs of Ascents collection is designed to produce. "LORD, my heart is not proud (lo-gavah libbi); my eyes are not raised in arrogance (ve'lo-ramu einai); I do not involve myself with matters too great or too wonderful (beniflaut) for me" — the three negations define humility through its opposite: pride of heart (the inner disposition), elevated eyes (the visible arrogance posture), and involvement in matters beyond one's proper sphere. The "matters too wonderful (niflaut)" may refer to divine mysteries, cosmic decisions, or the political and theological questions that belong to YHWH rather than to human calculation. The humble person recognizes the sphere of human competence and remains within it.
2: "No, I have settled and silenced my soul (shivviti ve'domamti nafshi); like a weaned child (kegamul) resting contentedly with its mother — like a weaned child, my soul is with me" — the weaned child image is the psalm's center and requires careful reading. The gamul (weaned child, one who has been weaned) is specifically not the nursing infant who cries for milk — it is the child who has already passed through the weaning crisis (which in antiquity occurred around ages 2-3, when the child was abruptly separated from the breast) and arrived at a settled contentment without demanding to nurse. This is not passivity or infant dependency but achieved tranquility: the child has worked through the crisis of being denied what it most wanted and found peace on the other side. The psalmist has done the same: the soul (nefesh) that once cried out demanding divine answers has been weaned and now rests quietly — not in ignorance but in trust.
3: "O Israel, put your hope in the LORD both now and forever (me'attah ve'ad-olam)" — the tiny psalm closes with the same corporate exhortation as Ps 130:7 ("O Israel, put your hope in the LORD") and with the same temporal formula as Ps 121:8 ("now and forever"). The individual achievement of the weaned-child tranquility (v2) becomes the model for the corporate community's hope. What the psalmist has personally achieved — the silencing and settling of the demanding soul — is offered to the whole covenant community as the goal of the pilgrimage journey. The Songs of Ascents that began with the cry of distress in "Meshech and Kedar" (Ps 120:5) have traveled through fear, arrival, petition, thanksgiving, and penitential depth to arrive here: the settled, trusting soul that rests in YHWH without demands. Israel is called to this same achieved peace.
